items.q: After two half-lives, how much of a radioactive sample remains undecayed?
items.correct: One quarter — each half-life halves whatever is left.
items.misconception: None — two halves means it has all decayed.
items.why: Students add 1/2 + 1/2 instead of halving the remainder each time; decay is exponential, not linear.

items.q: Is food that has been irradiated to sterilise it radioactive afterwards?
items.correct: No — irradiation transfers energy but no radioactive material; contamination is what makes things radioactive.
items.misconception: Yes — anything exposed to radiation becomes radioactive.
items.random_wrong: Yes — but only food in metal cans.
items.why: Students conflate irradiation with contamination, which fuels unnecessary fear of sterilised food and medical equipment.

items.q: Can you predict when an individual nucleus will decay?
items.correct: No — decay is random; only the average behaviour of large numbers of nuclei is predictable, via the half-life.
items.misconception: Yes — each nucleus decays exactly when one half-life has passed.
items.random_wrong: Yes — heating the sample sets the decay time.
items.why: The regularity of half-life misleads students into thinking individual decays happen on a schedule.

items.q: Which type of radiation is stopped by a sheet of paper?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Alpha. “X-rays” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: The same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
items.random_wrong: Different numbers of protons but the same neutrons.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.. “No neutrons at all.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In the nuclide notation ²³⁸₉₂U, the number 92 is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The proton (atomic) number. “The number of neutrons” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: A random, spontaneous process unaffected by temperature or pressure.
items.why: It's A random, spontaneous process unaffected by temperature or pressure.. “Caused by chemical reactions.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A sample's activity is 800 Bq and its half-life is 10 minutes. What is the activity after 20 minutes?
items.why: The real figure is 200 Bq. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
